channel.title: Anicca on Early Reflections
channel.description: Recent content in Anicca on Early Reflections

item.title: Akrasia and the Bodhisattva Ideal
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
A familiar stereotype underlying the pejorative ‘Hinayana’ framing of the non-Mahayana schools is the monk who withdraws from the world to selfishly pursue enlightenment for themselves. According to this framing the Mahayana distinguishes itself from the Hinayana through its emphasis on compassion as the primary goal of the path.

This stereotype is undermined somewhat by the fact that even according to the most world-transcending early portrayals of nirvana, the cultivation of moral perfection, which certainly includes compassion, is regarded as necessary for enlightenment. Any monk who was genuinely withdrawn from the suffering of others would thereby be incapable of achieving enlightenment, by Hinayana lights too. In terms of outcomes, then, there appears to be little difference between the two vehicles.
item.content:encoded:
A familiar stereotype underlying the pejorative ‘Hinayana’ framing of the non-Mahayana schools is the monk who withdraws from the world to selfishly pursue enlightenment for themselves. According to this framing the Mahayana distinguishes itself from the Hinayana through its emphasis on compassion as the primary goal of the path.

This stereotype is undermined somewhat by the fact that even according to the most world-transcending early portrayals of nirvana, the cultivation of moral perfection, which certainly includes compassion, is regarded as necessary for enlightenment. Any monk who was genuinely withdrawn from the suffering of others would thereby be incapable of achieving enlightenment, by Hinayana lights too. In terms of outcomes, then, there appears to be little difference between the two vehicles.

Nevertheless there is clearly something at stake in the Boddhisattva ideal. My view is that the dispute really concerns a more foundational metaethical question, namely: why should we be moral at all? If the Hinayana answer is that you should cultivate moral concern for others because it will help you achieve enlightenment, then the Mahayana could be interpreted as rejecting this answer, instead urging that moral concern must be cultivated as an end in itself.

Given outcomes are the same, you could be forgiven for wondering why this question matters. But while theoretically the destination may be the same, the answer may have important implications for how moral motivation works in practice. Indeed, the whole reason it might seem like a good idea to find self-interested reasons to cultivate moral virtue is that they provide a practitioner with concrete, personal reasons to pursue a moral project which may otherwise seem abstract. Perhaps, then, we can see the Boddhisattva ideal as arising out of a growing suspicion that this strategy is flawed. More explicitly, the suspicion might be that self-interested motivations for cultivating moral virtue are self-defeating.

Why might we believe this? One immediate reason is that life routinely throws up situations in which self-interest and moral concern are at odds with one another. Even armed with the observation that the cultivation of moral concern makes us happier (a fact now confirmed by empirical psychology), this logic has limits—many situations arise in which morality demands a degree of self-sacrifice impossible to justify in terms of some hypothetical renumeration in happiness or spiritual growth. In fact it is only in these situations we’d typically consider there to be something genuinely moral on the line.

But there’s another, more fundamental reason to reject self-interested justifications for morality. To illustrate, consider a response to the above problem based on a conception of ‘expanded’ self-interest. We might think here of Vedic conceptions of self in which one’s own personal identity is equated (via the intermediary notion of Atman) with the whole of reality or Brahman. On this view moral concern for others is justified on the basis that they already are you, in some suitably foundational and metaphysical sense. From the perspective of the ultimate, their needs and preferences are subsumed into your own sphere of prudential self-interest.

This route may seem promising, as it seems to offer a simple and direct reason to care about the suffering of others. But consider how it might regard conflicts of interest. Since according to this model a conflict of interest between two people is (from the ultimate perspective) a conflict between two parts of the same self, the situation is analogous to a conflict of desire within a single person. Say I decide to quit smoking on Monday, then on Friday find myself reaching for a cigarette. How do I decide which desire to act on? One approach is to suggest there can only be one ‘true’ desire, and resolving the conflict is simply a matter of improving one’s self-knowledge. But this amounts to the claim that the conflict is illusory, a position which if dubious in the case of conflicting desire will only become more so when widened to moral conflict in general.

Another common approach is to view resolution of the conflict as a matter of will-power. But this misunderstands the problem, which is not about the relative strength of the conflicting desires. It is rather a logical problem arising from the fact that there is only one locus of account. By recognising my desire to stop smoking on Monday I effectively place myself under a rule: to refrain from smoking even if the desire arises. The problem is that the same authority with which I bind myself to that rule can then be used to nullify or reinterpret it later. So when the desire to smoke inevitably does arise I have no real reason to discount it in favour of my original desire to quit. Viewed this way, it can be seen that the problem is not so much that my capacity to self-legislate is too weak, but that it is too strong. In practice, if the force of a reason (to act or refrain from acting) derives from immediate self-legislation then it seems we will never have good reason to do anything other than act on whichever desire is immediately present. This is regardless of whether it conflicts with other desires we’ve had at other times, or with higher aspirations we may associate with our sense of ourselves as beings extended in time.

This has sometimes been called the problem of akrasia. This word was first used by philosophers in ancient Greece who were perplexed about how it is possible that a person could ever act against their own better judgement. But what the above line of thought actually points to is the opposite problem, namely how it is possible that anyone ever restrains their immediate impulses in the pursuit of long term goals. The lesson usually taken from this argument—which originated with Wittgenstein and was later developed by Saul Kripke1—is that the normative force of a rule, and hence of reasons in general, always depends on its being articulated within a community of rule followers. If there is only a single rule follower the content of the rule will be inherently unstable. What seems to be required is the recognition of others’ authority to hold you to the commitments you take upon yourself.

This insight into the social nature of rule-following and the normative force of reasons both shows why an Atman-based account of expanded self-interest cannot provide an adequate response to the metaethical question, and points the way to a better answer. The problem is that by assimilating moral concern into the prudential sphere of a single universal self it generates a problem analogous to akrasia, in effect guaranteeing that genuine moral conflicts can never be resolved in practice (parties to a conflict can never provide one another with a reason to compromise). But while it shows that self-interest cannot be used to underpin moral concern, even when universalised, it also hints that the opposite may be possible. If our capacity to take ourselves as autonomous rational subjects always depends on our embedding within a community who mutually reinforce and stabilise the content of the rules we each place on ourselves, then this indicates that something like a moral sense—the mutual recognition of distinct others as ends in themselves—is logically prior to any desires, preferences or goals indexed to individual, temporally extended selves.

Let’s come back to the Bodhisattva ideal. I’ve suggested that this development within the Buddhist tradition might be seen as the rejection of a certain answer to a distinctively metaethical question. But once looked at this way, we can also see that it doesn’t really provide its own answer. If asked why we should be moral, the Bodhisattva ideal just says yes. The line of thought I’ve been exploring here gestures towards a more substantial answer to this question. This answer is broadly Kantian: it says that the authority of morality derives directly from the structure of our own rational agency. More explicitly, it says that insofar as we take ourselves to have any reasons at all—including our own self-interested desires and preferences—we are already implicitly committed to moral reasons to act or refrain from acting, whether or not we acknowledge this commitment explicitly. The further (ultimately Hegelian) insight that practical agency is a social achievement makes explicit how the moral sense—the recognition of other sentient beings as sources of reasons which obligate your action—is logically prior to rational self-interest.

  1. Introduced in the Philosophical Investigations [1], Wittgenstein first presented this as an argument intended to show that a language spoken by just one person is impossible. It was later developed into a general rule-following paradox by Kripke in Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language [2]. ↩︎

item.title: From Impermanence to Emptiness
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: In this post I want to jot down a few thoughts on the relationship between impermanence and emptiness. This is inspired by some comments Rob Burbea makes in Seeing That Frees1, which came as something of a revelation as they articulate clearly something I’ve felt at an intuitive but murky level for some time. These concern the limitations of using impermanence as a primary framework for insight, and how a reorientation around emptiness can help move beyond these limitations. Implicitly, then, this is about issues right at the root of the origins of the Mahayana. But in typical Burbea fashion it is framed in a way that has direct relevance for practice.
item.content:encoded:
In this post I want to jot down a few thoughts on the relationship between impermanence and emptiness. This is inspired by some comments Rob Burbea makes in Seeing That Frees1, which came as something of a revelation as they articulate clearly something I’ve felt at an intuitive but murky level for some time. These concern the limitations of using impermanence as a primary framework for insight, and how a reorientation around emptiness can help move beyond these limitations. Implicitly, then, this is about issues right at the root of the origins of the Mahayana. But in typical Burbea fashion it is framed in a way that has direct relevance for practice.

In some traditional framings of the three characteristics of conditioned existence, impermanence is presented as the master concept that explains the other two. Phenomena are understood to be unsatisfactory because they are impermanent. Anattā is understood as the recognition that there is no fixed or permanent self. But on closer inspection these understandings may appear a little flimsy. Does craving really depend on a perception of permanence? If I feel the need to reach for a beer after work, is the force of this compulsion really predicated on a sense—conscious or unconscious—that any pleasure or relief it might provide will persist beyond the immediate future? It is true that observing a desire as impermanent and subject to change can loosen its grip, but this observation can cut both ways. I have certainly had experiences where bringing the standpoint of impermanence to craving has actually seemed to increase its solidity and momentum—after all, if the craving is impermanent then so are the consequences of acting on it.

Another example can help pin down what’s at stake in this. Say you have a romantic infatuation that drags on and on. For various reasons—perhaps it is not reciprocated, or is reciprocated inconsistently—it becomes a whole cursed tangle of a thing, involving much dissatisfaction and pain. But one day it just kind of lifts, like the curtains at the end of a long and tragic play, and you are left looking at this person thinking ugh, this is just a normal plain old boring person with normal problems and normal warts. What was I thinking?

You could look at this experience through the lens of impermanence. The story here might be something like: there was an object of desire and then separate to that there was a desire, and while the desire may have seemed very solid and important while it was present in the end it was just a passing cloud. But while this wouldn’t be wrong, it does seem to miss something crucial in this experience. Because it is not just that the desire passes while the object of desire remains the same—in this moment when the curtain lifts, the object of desire is itself revealed to be something other than it had been taken to be. The real revelation here is that the object of desire was always a construct of sorts, never just this particular person but also your own mental imputation externalised in them. If the deep structure of clinging involves this kind of fabrication of the very objects which are clung to, then an impermanence way of looking that doesn’t explicitly target this fabrication will have limited application in uncovering and unbinding clinging in its gross and subtle forms.

And it is this point which moves us into the terrain of emptiness, because insight into emptiness consists precisely in this recognition of the fabricated nature of perceptual objects. To say that the self as such is a fabrication devoid of substantial basis is to say something much stronger than that it is impermanent and subject to change. This is not just philosophical nitpicking, but potentially has deep implications for how the path itself is interpreted. There is, for instance, a not uncommon idea of spiritual development which is explicitly predicated on the idea that the self is not fixed or permanent—it is, therefore, something that can change and grow. Yet this idea can nevertheless operate under a more subtle kind of reification of self, in which the various dimensions of skill, experience, and integration involved in practice are implicitly collapsed down into a single developmental scale. Such an idea can produce a sense that, say, proficiency in meditation and insight should also be markers for other forms of psychological integration. This idea may not be endorsed explicitly but may rather operate in the background, as when people find it hard to understand how a dharma teacher who is undoubtedly very wise and experienced could also be implicated in sexual misconduct.

It might be argued that the impermanence framing already incorporates the notion that phenomena lack substantial basis, because to posit a substantial basis is to posit an unchanging medium in which the various changing properties of an object are unified. But to make this argument is to interpret impermanence through the lens of emptiness. This is certainly not a bad thing, and indeed it helps us to see how an impermanence way of looking might fit within a broader emptiness practice.

But when impermanence becomes the core focus of practice things can take quite a different direction. It is well documented that attending to the aspect of change in experience can produce a refinement of attention into an ever more fine-grained, microscopic beam which hones in on the most minute detail of experience. The standpoint of impermanence can in this way be heightened into a standpoint of momentariness, where something like a ‘framerate’ of consciousness starts to become apparent—a direction of practice which has been emphasised within some vipassana traditions. This is undoubtedly fascinating, not least because the deconstruction of the sense of duration that can happen at this level of detail (or so I’m told) has resonance with findings from contemporary cognitive neuroscience, which concern how the sense of a phenomenal ‘now’ is constructed from multiple distinct brain processes representing non-concurrent sensory data as occurring within a single window of simultaneity. That it might be possible in meditation to refine attention to such a degree that it cuts beneath the construction of the phenomenal now is nothing short of mind-blowing. Yet it is also reasonable to wonder how any of this may help to unbind clinging.

As a final thought I’ll briefly consider the implications of these insight frames for the way we may think about samādhi and metta. The idea that an impermanence way of looking leads naturally to microscopic attention pairs with a corresponding conception of samādhi as laser-like one-pointed focus. While such a state certainly can be viewed as one kind of samādhi, to identify it with samādhi would seem to create problems. For example it would mystify the relationship between samādhi and metta, because after all what does laser focus have to do with loving-kindness? If we reorient to an emptiness way of looking, however, we might instead conceive samādhi simply as the collectedness or unification of mind that we naturally rest into when we fabricate less. To use another of Rob Burbea’s useful phrases, samādhi is just the other face of letting go. One great advantage of this way of seeing things is that the relationship between samādhi and metta suddenly becomes clear. The cultivation of loving-kindness is simply a direct means of fabricating less self. In this way metta and samādhi emerge as two aspects of a single process, which the standpoint of emptiness reveals as the process of defabrication.

  1. Specifically, in Chapter 12. [1] ↩︎

item.title: Impermanence, Impermanence, Impermanence
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: While all of the three marks of existence present their own conceptual difficulties, anicca (impermanence) has always seemed to me the most slippery, particularly where insight is concern. This is not because it is harder to comprehend than dukkha or anattā (unsatisfactoriness or no-self), but for the opposite reason that it is almost too obvious. We already know that relationships end, that we will die some day, that whims come and go, that tectonic plates drift and that a clay pot left in the ground for a few thousand years will crumble and decay. We also know that if we watch something long enough we will see it change. What, then, can insight into impermanence really consist in?
item.content:encoded:
While all of the three marks of existence present their own conceptual difficulties, anicca (impermanence) has always seemed to me the most slippery, particularly where insight is concern. This is not because it is harder to comprehend than dukkha or anattā (unsatisfactoriness or no-self), but for the opposite reason that it is almost too obvious. We already know that relationships end, that we will die some day, that whims come and go, that tectonic plates drift and that a clay pot left in the ground for a few thousand years will crumble and decay. We also know that if we watch something long enough we will see it change. What, then, can insight into impermanence really consist in?

One thought is that insight into impermanence is not so much about observing elements of experience as impermanent, but about observing the way that our mind imbues the objects of experience with artificial permanence. Insight will thus consist, at least in part, of the recognition of a gap between the way we intuitively treat things and the way we know them to be. It could be argued that those of us born into the post-scientific world with our knowledge of thermodynamics, evolution by natural selection and cosmic expansion, are better placed to grasp impermanence than anyone has ever been. But this would just mean that the persistence of those default attitudes, which represent the world as infused with false permanence, should be all the more surprising—an even more fertile ground for insight.

Everyday cravings and aversions, for instance, seem to involve a perception of at least relative permanence. The form of attachment distinctive of craving manifests as a certain ‘solidification’ of desire, a projection of temporal persistent. A desire perceived as temporally persistent is one that demands to be satisfied, in that it may seem as if the only way to ease the discomfort accompanying an unsatisfied desire is by acquiring its object. That this is a projection of permanence is reflected in the fact that it gains force only by masking another means of easing of discomfort, namely by waiting out the desire. Left alone a desire can be observed to play out by itself and eventually to fade away. By directly noticing the impermanence of desire in this way, insight can filter into intuition and loosen the grip of craving.

The principle at work here is that impermanence is essentially self-revealing, in the sense that the only thing required to intuitively grasp something’s impermanence is to consistently bring attention to it. The converse of this point is that those things we take (falsely) to be permanent will be those we do not tend to bring attention to. Those things we technically know to be impermanent but which remain out of mind could be said to form the intuitively ‘stable’ background of life, against which the objects of our immediate attention dart and flicker. This picture goes some way to explaining why disruptions to that background, events which may seem inevitable in retrospect—the death of a close relative, a sudden deterioration of health—can seem at the time like an intrusion of the unthinkable. This points to a second, perhaps deeper form that insight into impermanence can take: the direct noticing of background elements as belonging to the background. Such a noticing implies the movement of an element from the background into the foreground of awareness, which will ensure its stability is revealed to intuition (not just to knowledge) as illusory.

Pushing this idea to its logical conclusion yields a third level of insight, concerning the universal scope of impermanence. Having engaged in this practice persistently, bringing attention to background elements which are thereby revealed to intuition as impermanent, we can imagine that we might begin to gain an intuitive sense of the impermanence not just of particular things, but of every thing. This has implications for the way we perceive change in general, because it is part of our natural way of seeing things that change is perceived as something that happens only relative to a fixed background. But what the universal scope of impermanence implies is that there is no fixed background, only impermanent phenomena unfolding at different rates.

A vivid example of how this might affect our intuitive experience is provided by an argument that originates with Aristotle, and that was an important source of resistance to the Copernican model of the solar system in the 1500s. This was the so-called ‘tower argument,’ which was taken for a long time to definitively uphold the Ptolemaic model according to which celestial objects revolve around a stationary Earth. This argument pointed to the fact that a rock dropped 1 meter from the top of a tower falls directly to the ground and lands 1 meter from its base. If the Earth were in motion, so the reasoning went, then the tower would have moved by the time the rock landed, and it would hit the ground at a different distance from its base.

This reasoning is now recognised as fallacious, and one crucial aspect of Galileo’s campaign to promote Copernicanism was his illustration of what goes wrong with it. He realised that the tower argument depends on the assumption that motion is absolute, which is to say all motion occurs relative to a universal frame of reference (an ultimate fixed background). Underwritten by this implicit principle is the assumption that except in clear-cut instances of illusion, apparent motion coincides with real motion (and therefore that apparent stability coincides with real stability). In developing what is now known as Galilean relativity—in which all motion is conceived as relative and there is no universal frame of reference—Galileo in effect undermined this assumption, and with it the intuitive force of the tower argument.1

The fundamental point here is a counter-intuitive one: that once the idea that change is always change relative to a fixed background has been rejected—once we appreciate that our perception of change is only ever perception of change relative to things which are themselves changing, i.e. that impermanence is a true universal—we should expect the illusion of stability to be ubiquitous. While the analogy is somewhat impressionistic, in this example of Galilean relativity and the tower argument we catch a glimpse of how knowledge of impermanence, which in the first place may seem obvious, can ultimately yield insight into the fundamental nature of how reality is constructed in experience, so long as it is realised at the level of intuition and applied universally.

  1. A wonderful philosophical account of the conceptual shifts involved in the Copernican revolution can be found in Paul Feyerabend’s classic Against Method [1]. ↩︎
